channel.title: Daily Feed (Last 48 Hours, Updated 9 AM BD)
channel.description: Daily Feed (Last 48 Hours, Updated 9 AM BD) generated by script

item.title: China Wants to Regulate AI Companions
item.description: With the latest rules, Beijing hopes to rein in a booming industry.

item.title: Trump Pivots His Strait of Hormuz Strategy—Again
item.description: Pressure from Gulf leaders prompted Trump to abandon proposed tolls.

item.title: The Gulf States That Iran Thinks It Can Attack
item.description: Why Bahrain and Kuwait have become Tehran’s preferred targets.

item.title: When China’s open-source AI is a trap
item.description: America’s quest for AI dominance is scary. China is not the solution

item.title: Cities are rethinking what happens after dark
item.description: Making it easier to party is becoming serious public policy

item.title: China Is Ready to Get Tough
item.description: Beijing’s diplomatic retaliation has limits—but fewer than before.

item.title: The Houthis Have Joined the Chat
item.description: Iran’s break-the-glass plan to strangle another key waterway may now be active.

item.title: Trump’s Hormuz brinkmanship is worsening a global fuel crunch
item.description: Rising oil prices are only part of the problem

item.title: What investment gurus get wrong
item.description: To see a country’s financial follies, look to its celebrity advisers

item.title: What investment gurus get wrong
item.description: To see a country’s financial follies, look to its celebrity advisers

item.title: Haunted houses are in demand in Japan
item.description: Why tenants are coming round to “incident properties”?

item.title: Asia’s Real Energy Test
item.description: The Iran war has underscored the fragility of global energy markets, exposing which economies can withstand repeated shocks and which cannot. For ASEAN+3 countries, resilience will depend on stronger electricity systems, regional cooperation, and institutions that can manage persistent uncertainty.

item.title: The US Economy Cannot Grow Without Renewables
item.description: Consumption demand, which has long powered America’s formidable economy, rests on the promise of cheap energy. But the Trump administration has conflated that with fossil fuels, confusing historic correlation with causation and, in the process, jeopardizing long-term prosperity.

item.title: Will a U.S.-Iran Deal Include Kurds?
item.description: Attacks against Kurdish regions in Iran and Iraq never stopped.

item.title: The Purpose of Development Cooperation Is to Be Unnecessary
item.description: The original promise of development cooperation was to help countries overcome poverty, build productive economies, and reduce their dependence on external resources. That has not happened, and ongoing reviews of development cooperation, which focus on donor countries' needs and priorities, are unlikely to change this.

item.title: Analysing Africa newsletter: Another looming atrocity in Sudan
item.description: John McDermott, our chief Africa correspondent, argues that the conflict is not “forgotten” but “neglected”

item.title: Modi’s Visit: Australia, India Seek to Gain Strength From Each Other
item.description: Australia and India are framing new minilateral coalitions to maximize their strategic options.

item.title: Donald Trump has no good options for reopening the Strait of Hormuz
item.description: Yet the stand-off has costs for cash-strapped Iran, too

item.title: Oil Shocks Are No Longer So Shocking
item.description: Despite this year's US/Israeli-Iran war causing the largest-ever disruption to global oil supplies, the shocks of the 1970s had a greater impact. Because oil has been used as a weapon for so long, markets, policymakers, and strategists have adapted.

item.title: It’s Time for Australia to Be a Good Neighbor to the Pacific
item.description: Australia’s Pacific approach equates to long stretches of neglect punctuated by spurts of anxiety and activity. That's no way to build durable security.

item.title: The Pentagon Needs $1.5 Trillion
item.description: At a perilous moment, Washington is smart to invest in a bigger defense budget.

item.title: As China Claims Philippines’ Batanes, Taiwan Emphasizes Kinship
item.description: Historical links between the Indigenous peoples of Taiwan and the Philippines are dovetailing with present-day geopolitical concerns.

item.title: Pakistan’s Ahmadiyya Community Is Being Persecuted in Plain Sight
item.description: Extremist-led mob violence in Pakistan often leads to further persecution – at the hands of the state.

item.title: AOC Meets World
item.description: The congresswoman’s rise was powered mostly by domestic issues. But she is slowly expanding to foreign affairs.

item.title: Will Sheikh Hasina’s Planned Homecoming Revive the Awami League?
item.description: Instead of being seen as a fugitive from justice, the former Bangladeshi prime minister could present herself as a political leader willing to face trial in her own country.

item.title: Demis Hassabis has a plan to harness AI safely
item.description: The Google Deepmind co-founder sets out his vision in an exclusive interview

item.title: Sri Lanka Prison Riot Exposes Intertwined Crises of Drugs, Crime and Overcrowding
item.description: A recent prison riot at Negombo underscores the need for the government to dismantle drug trafficking networks, contain criminal activity and carry out prison reforms.

item.title: The War Room newsletter: Ukraine’s Game of Drones
item.description: Oliver Carroll, our Ukraine correspondent, assesses the performance of the country’s defence minister

item.title: Myanmar’s Leader Min Aung Hlaing to Visit Thailand Next Month, Report Claims
item.description: The visit comes amid a campaign by Naypyidaw to normalize its relations with the Association of Southeast Asian Nations (ASEAN) and its member states.

item.title: Putin Will Turn a Cease-Fire Into a Weapon
item.description: A new use for Russia’s old playbook in Ukraine.

item.title: How China Is Winning Friends and Influencing People
item.description: Private companies are making Beijing look good in the developing world.

item.title: Antonia Mulvey on the Pursuit of Justice in Myanmar
item.description: "In the short term, the likelihood of Myanmar’s military leadership appearing before a court is low, but not impossible."

item.title: Indonesia’s Capital Problems
item.description: The country needs something that works better than Jakarta, but at what cost?

item.title: Ratings Agency Maintains Indonesia Credit Rating, Predicting Economic Recovery
item.description: The news will no doubt come as a relief for the Prabowo administration, whose economic policymaking has spooked international investors.

item.title: India’s Expanding SSBN Force and What It Means for Pakistan
item.description: As India’s expanding fleet of Arihant-class subs improves its second-strike capability, Islamabad has to pursue the development and deployment of its own SSBN force.

item.title: Ukraine’s army gets a performance review
item.description: New defence minister Mykhailo Fedorov is trying to reform the army. The generals are unimpressed

item.title: Ukraine’s reforming defence minister is under fire
item.description: Mykhailo Fedorov has been trying to reform the army. The generals are pushing back

item.title: Modi’s New Zealand Visit Closed a 40-Year Gap
item.description: Modi’s visit signaled India’s willingness to strengthen ties with New Zealand, despite the geographical distance.

item.title: Far More Than Ruins: Life in Engilcheck, Kyrgyzstan
item.description: The ruins of Engilcheck, a Soviet-era mining boomtown in the Tien Shan mountains, are still home to a small but thriving community.

item.title: Examining the Evolving Virtual Front of the Southern Thailand Insurgency
item.description: Supporters of the separatist movement are using social media to reinforce grievances, propagate resistance narratives, and sustain support for the insurgency.
